did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the K-Nearest Neighbor (K-NN) machine learning algorithm for classification. It extracted ABCD features (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) from skin images using image processing techniques and applied K-NN to classify Basal Cell Carcinoma nodular versus Dermatofibroma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of an Android application for early detection of Basal Cell Carcinoma using ABCD feature extraction and K-Nearest Neighbor classification. It includes a specific research methodology, system design, implementation of feature extraction algorithms, and experimental results (e.g., accuracy rates of 85% and 69%) on a dataset of skin images. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICAMIMIA), it reports new technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Early Detection Of Skin Basal Cell Carcinoma Nodular Using Extraction Methods ABCD Feature Comparison With Dermatofibroma Data
journal: 2019 International Conference on Advanced Mechatronics, Intelligent Manufacture and Industrial Automation (ICAMIMIA)
brief_summary: This study developed an Android-based application for the early detection of nodular Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) by distinguishing it from Dermatofibroma using image processing and machine learning. The system extracts ABCD features (Asymmetry, Border, Color, and Diameter) from skin images and classifies them using the K-Nearest Neighbor (K-NN) algorithm. The research involved testing the system under various environmental conditions, including different lighting intensities (100-500 lux), shooting distances (5 cm), and angles (0 degrees). Experimental results indicated that the system could diagnose the potential for BCC with an accuracy rate of 69% under optimal conditions. The study concluded that image resolution and standardized capture parameters significantly influence classification accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study, specifically the nodular subtype, distinguished from Dermatofibroma using ABCD feature extraction and K-NN classification.
Used as the comparator class to distinguish from Basal Cell Carcinoma in the classification task.

Mentioned in the introduction as a common skin cancer type in Indonesia and in the context of related work on ABCD feature extraction, but not part of the experimental dataset or classification task of this specific study.
Mentioned in the introduction as a common skin cancer type in Indonesia, but not part of the experimental dataset or classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category: Basal Cell Carcinoma (nodular) vs. Dermatofibroma
all_task_types.details: The paper uses the K-Nearest Neighbor (K-NN) algorithm to classify skin images into two discrete diagnostic categories: Basal Cell Carcinoma (labeled as 1 or 'sick') and Dermatofibroma (labeled as 0 or 'healthy'). The classification is based on extracted ABCD features (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter). The study reports accuracy rates of 85% and 69% for this binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: Custom Collected Dataset (Indoor Captures)
datasets_used.description: A dataset of skin images captured indoors using an Android application under controlled conditions (lighting, distance, angle) to distinguish Basal Cell Carcinoma from Dermatofibroma. The images were processed to extract ABCD features.

use.training: The paper mentions that 'training results will be used during the testing phase' and references a 'training process' for K-NN, but does not specify a separate training set count or split for the main experimental results. The 86 images appear to be used for testing/validation of the system's performance under various conditions.
use.validation: Used to test the system's accuracy under varying environmental conditions (lux, distance, angle).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification accuracy of the Android application. The paper reports 100% accuracy on this specific partition of 86 identical training/testing samples, and 69% accuracy when testing under varying environmental conditions.
use.other: The paper also references a separate study [6] that used 30 dermatoscopic images for melanoma diagnosis, but this was not the dataset used for the primary BCC vs. Dermatofibroma classification in this study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification accuracy rates (85% and 69%) and observed effects of environmental conditions (lux, distance, angle) on performance without statistical significance testing.

Custom Collected Dataset (Indoor Capture.notes: The study tested the system under varying environmental conditions. Optimal results were achieved with 100-500 lux lighting, 5 cm shooting distance, and 0 degree shooting angle. Image resolution was identified as a key factor influencing classification accuracy.

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: ABCD Feature Extraction (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter)

training_details.training_method: Instance-based (no explicit model training phase; uses training data for distance calculation during inference)

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Class label with the highest frequency among the k nearest neighbors

conclusions: The developed Android-based system using ABCD feature extraction and K-Nearest Neighbor (K-NN) classification can diagnose the potential for nodular Basal Cell Carcinoma with an accuracy rate of 69% under optimal environmental conditions.
Optimal classification accuracy is achieved when images are captured with lighting intensity between 100-500 lux, a shooting distance of 5 cm, and a shooting angle of 0 degrees.
Image resolution and standardized capture parameters (lighting, distance, angle) significantly influence the classification accuracy of the system.

The study acknowledges that the current dataset consists of simulated or non-direct patient samples and recommends future research to use direct samples from patients suffering from basal cell carcinoma.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that the dataset consists of simulated or non-direct patient samples, limiting clinical applicability; future work is recommended to use direct samples from patients.
The system achieved 100% accuracy on a partition of 86 identical training/testing samples, which indicates potential data leakage or overfitting due to lack of independent test data.
Under varying environmental conditions (lighting, distance, angle), the classification accuracy dropped significantly to 69%, highlighting sensitivity to capture parameters.
Image resolution and standardized capture parameters (100-500 lux lighting, 5 cm distance, 0 degree angle) were identified as critical factors influencing accuracy.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the reported accuracy rates.
The dataset size is small (86 images total), and the paper does not specify a separate validation set, relying instead on testing under varying environmental conditions.
